concepts.title: Halides MX2 and MX4
concepts.conceptBody:
Halides $\mathbf{M}{{\mathbf{X}}_{\mathbf{2}}}$ and $\mathbf{M}{{\mathbf{X}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$

Group - 14 elements form tetrahalides of the type $\text{M}{{\text{X}}_{4}}(\text{X}=\text{F},\text{Cl},\text{Br},\text{I})$ except $\text{Pb}{{\text{I}}_{4}}$.

$\text{Pb}{{\text{I}}_{4}}$ does not exist due to strong oxidizing power of $\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{+4}}$ and reducing nature of ${{\text{I}}^{-}}$ion. The $\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{+4}}$ is strong oxidizing agent due to greater stability of $\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{+2}}$ (inert pair effect).

Thus, $\text{M}{{\text{X}}_{4}},[\text{M}=\text{C},\text{Si},\text{Ge},\text{Sn},\text{X}=\text{F},\text{Cl},\text{Br},\text{I}]$

Germanium, $\text{Sn}$ and $\text{Pb}$ also for $\text{M}{{\text{X}}_{2}}$. The stability of $\text{M}{{\text{X}}_{2}}$ is maximum for $\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{+2}}$.

$\text{Sn}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$ is sparingly soluble in water and is used in fluoride containing tooth pastes.

The $\text{M}{{\text{X}}_{4}}$ halides are all colourless except $\text{Ge}{{\text{I}}_{4}}$ and $\text{Sn}{{\text{I}}_{4}}$ which are bright orange solids. Colour in these halides is due to charge transfer.

All tetrachlorides except $\text{CC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$ are hydrolysed by water.

Carbon tetrachloride is not hydrolysed because:

(a) carbon in $\text{CC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$ is coordinatively saturated

(b) $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond is less polar

${{\text{X}}_{\text{C}}}=2.5,{{\text{X}}_{\text{Cl}}}=3.0,\Delta \chi =0.5$

Silicon tetra chloride $\left( \text{SiC}{{\text{l}}_{4}} \right)$ is extensively hydrolysed. It is because:

(a) $\text{Si}-\text{Cl}$ bond is more polar

${{\text{X}}_{\text{Si}}}=1.8,{{\text{X}}_{\text{Cl}}}=3.0,\Delta \chi =1.2$

(b) $\text{Si}$ in $\text{SiC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$ can increase its coordination number by using $3~\text{d}$ orbitals

The tetra halides of $\text{Si},\text{Ge},\text{Sn}$ and $\text{Pb}$ can increase their coordination numbers by using d orbitals and can form haloanions.

$\text{Si}{{\text{F}}_{4}}+2~{{\text{F}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{SiF}_{6}^{2-}$

$\text{ SnC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}+2\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{SnCl}_{6}^{-}$

The hexacoordinated halo anions are octahedral having $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{3}}~{{\text{d}}^{2}}$ hybridised central atom (Si).

concepts.title: Hydrides of p block
concepts.conceptBody:
Hydrides:

All element of this group form hydrides.

Carbon forms a large number of chain and ring compounds due to high catenation property. They are:

Alkanes ${{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{H}}_{2\text{n}+2}}$

Alkenes ${{C}_{n}}{{H}_{2n}}$

Alkynes ${{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}{{\text{H}}_{2\text{n}-2}}$

Cyclo alkanes and cycloalkynes

Aromatic hydrocarbons $\left( {{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{6}} \right)$

Silicon (others also) form saturated hydrides only which are called silanes, $\text{S}{{\text{i}}_{n}}{{\text{H}}_{2\text{n}+2}}(\text{Si}\sim8)$.

$\text{SiC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}+\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Si}{{\text{H}}_{4}}+\text{AlC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+\text{LiCl}$

concepts.title: Silicones
concepts.conceptBody:
Silicons:

They are organosilicon polymers having empirical formula approximately ${{\text{R}}_{2}}\text{SiO}$. Polymers are very stable due to strong $\text{Si}-\text{O}$ and $\text{Si}-\text{C}$ bonds. Silicones can be prepared as oils, resins, plastics etc. They are:

$\to \quad $ Inert

$\to \quad $ Water repellent

$\to \quad $ Heat resistant

$\to \quad $ Electrical insulator

All these properties make silicones very useful.

Hydrolysis of alkyl chlorosilanes produces products having $\text{Si}-\text{OH}$ bonds. They do not dehydrate like gem diols ( of C)

concepts.title: Silicates
concepts.conceptBody:
Silicates

Silicates are solids having $\text{Si}-\text{O}$ bonds. Most of the rocks and even soil contain silicates. Alkali metal silicates are water soluble. They are made on a large scale as

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\sim 1500{}^\circ C}\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{4}}\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{4}},{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{\text{n}}}\text{ etc}\text{. }+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

They are used in detergents. The concentrated aq. solution of $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{4}}\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is solid like and as known as water glass. Variety of silicates are found but in all the basic unit is the $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ tetrahedron.

Each $\text{O}$ - atom carries one unit negative charge. In polymeric silicates $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ units share $\text{O}-$ atoms(s). It provides a way of classification.

Orthosilicates (Mononuclear silicates)

These are silicates having discrete $\text{SiO}_{4}^{4-}$ ions. Metal ions are surrounded by O-atoms.

Pyrosilicates $\mathbf{(S}{{\mathbf{i}}_{\mathbf{2}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{7}}}^{-\mathbf{6}}\mathbf{)}$:

When two $\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ units share only one $\text{O}-$ atom pyrosilicates, $\text{S}{{\text{i}}_{2}}\text{O}_{7}^{-6}$, are formed. These are also discrete units.

concepts.title: Glass
concepts.conceptBody:
Glass

Solids are most stable in crystalline form. However, if a solid is formed rapidly (for example, when a liquid is cooled quickly), its atoms or molecules do not have time to align themselves and may become locked in positions other than those of a regular crystal.

If we mix sodium carbonate and calcium carbonate with sand and fuse the mixture at about $1500\text{C}$, we get a liquid mixture of sodium and calcium silicates. When this mixture is cooled, it becomes viscous and eventually ceases to flow; it becomes solid -called glass. Glass is thus supercooled liquid (solid solution). Amorphous solids, such as glass, lack regular three dimensional arrangement of atoms. Glass thus refers to an optically transparent fusion product of inorganic materials that has cooled to a rigid state without crystallising.

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaSi}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

By varying the proportions of the three basic ingredients, and by adding other substances, we can alter the properties of the glass as given:

  • Calcium-alkali silicate glass - normal domestic glass for windows

Sodium silicate - soda glass used in cheap laboratory glassware

  • Fining agents such as $\text{NaN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ or $\text{A}{{\text{s}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ are added to remove bubbles.
  • Decolorising agents may be added to eliminate impurities and to obtain colourless glass. $\text{Ca}{{\text{F}}_{2}}$ is sometimes added to make opal glass.

Glass is attacked by HF and this property is used to make marking on the glass (etching)

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Si}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+6\text{HF}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{Si}{{\text{F}}_{6}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \text{ (in glass) }$

$\text{CaSi}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+6\text{HF}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaSi}{{\text{F}}_{6}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

HF, however does not attack wax. The portion of the glass on which etching is to be done is covered with wax layer. Markings are carved on the wax layer with a pin and HF is poured on the carved parts and left for sometime. After washing with water, wax is removed and permanent marking is obtained.
